topic: Applied Psychology: Issues in Mental Health
subject: psychology

items.q: Does 'statistically rare' always mean a behaviour is a mental disorder?
items.correct: No; rare traits such as very high intelligence are unusual but not disorders, so this definition is limited.
items.misconception: Any statistically rare behaviour is automatically abnormal and a disorder.
items.random_wrong: Statistical infrequency measures how dangerous a behaviour is.
items.why: Students assume rarity equals disorder, ignoring that many rare traits are positive or neutral.

items.q: Does the medical model see all mental disorders as purely biological?
items.correct: It emphasises biological causes and treatments but is criticised for downplaying psychological and social factors.
items.misconception: The medical model fully explains every disorder with biology alone.
items.random_wrong: The medical model rejects the use of any drug treatment.
items.why: Learners over-credit the medical model, ignoring criticisms about neglected social and psychological causes.

items.q: Have explanations of mental illness always been medical?
items.correct: No; historically they ranged from supernatural and moral views to today's medical and psychological models.
items.misconception: Mental illness has always been understood as a medical condition.
items.random_wrong: Mental illness was first discovered in the 21st century.
items.why: Students assume current medical understanding has always existed, missing the historical shift in views.

items.q: Defining abnormality as behaviour that is rare in the population is the:
items.random_wrong: Deviation from ideal mental health definition
items.why: It's Statistical infrequency definition. “Medical model” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The medical model explains mental disorders mainly as:
items.why: Correct answer: Illnesses with biological causes. “Entirely free choices” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Systems such as the DSM and ICD are used to:
items.why: It's Classify and diagnose mental disorders. “Treat all disorders with drugs” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Explaining depression in terms of low levels of serotonin is a:
items.why: It's Biological explanation. “Behaviourist explanation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
